# Hermes Workflows — authoring notes for LLMs

Practical, universal guidance for writing and operating Hermes workflow specs.
Applies to any workflow, independent of which project or board it touches.

## Scope decides how nodes execute

`scope` is not just metadata — it selects the node executor:

- `scope: global` → nodes run **direct** (in-process). No Kanban cards are
  created. Use this for read-only, maintenance, reporting, or
  fan-out/notify workflows that should not litter any board.
- `scope: project` (or `projects`) → every `agent_task` node is dispatched as a
  **Kanban worker card** on that project's board. Use this ONLY when the
  workflow genuinely drives cards through the board (e.g. an
  adopt/implement flow). Otherwise each run leaves worker cards behind and
  pollutes the board it was meant to serve.

Rule of thumb: if the workflow does not need a card per node, make it `global`
and reference everything by absolute path inside the prompts.

## Edges and branching

- An **unconditioned ("always") edge fires whenever the source node reaches
  `completed`, regardless of its outcome.** A node that soft-fails (reports
  `node_outcome: failure` but still completes) will therefore traverse an
  unconditioned edge and "fall through" to the next node. Treat `always` as a
  quick-prototyping crutch, not a finished design.
- Prefer an explicit condition on every edge out of a work node:
  `condition: {type: node_status, node: <source-id>, equals: success|failure}`.
  The `node` field is **required** for `node_status` conditions.
- Validator rule `incomplete_branch`: a node that has any `node_status`-
  conditioned outgoing edge must cover **both** `success` and `failure`, or else
  carry a `fallback: true` edge. Partial coverage fails validation. (This is why
  switching a single edge from "always" to "success" produces
  `incomplete_branch` — you must also wire the failure/fallback path.)
- `review_status` branches (human-review gates) MAY be partial: an unhandled
  decision intentionally stops the run.
- Route failures somewhere useful — typically a notifier node that reports the
  failure, then a terminal `finish` node with `outcome: failure`.

## Never change a live run's executor-determining fields

The engine **re-reads the spec on every advance**. Editing `scope` (or any field
that selects the executor) while a run is active switches the executor
mid-flight and orphans the in-flight run: a node backed by one executor can no
longer be reconciled by another, and the run freezes (e.g. a finished worker
card is never ingested, the node stays "scheduled" forever). Change such fields
only **between** runs. If you must unstick a run that was edited mid-flight,
temporarily restore the original field value so the engine can finish it, then
change it back once the run reaches a terminal node.

## Self-containment and portability

- Embed all instructions inline in node prompts. Do **not** write a node prompt
  that says "read /path/to/external-file and follow it" — that makes the
  workflow non-portable and dependent on host state.
- Custom scripts belong **in the workflow**, via a `script` node: materialize
  the script to a temp path with a heredoc inside the node's `command`, then have
  downstream agent nodes invoke it from that temp path. The workflow then carries
  its own copy and runs anywhere.
- When embedding a long script, generate the spec programmatically (read the
  script, prefix each line with the YAML block indent) rather than hand-pasting —
  YAML literal blocks strip the common indent, so the script's own indentation is
  preserved as long as you add the block indent uniformly. Verify the embedded
  copy round-trips byte-identical before trusting it.

## `script` node specifics

- Type `script`, required field `command` (a shell command; settles
  `success`/`failure` by exit code). Optional `cwd` and an `env` allowlist.
- **Gated by `execution.scripts_enabled` (default: false).** A workflow that
  contains any script node refuses to run until this is enabled. It is a
  security toggle (script nodes run arbitrary shell) and is global to all
  workflows — enable it deliberately.
- The script executor runs the command with an **allowlist-only environment**:
  only the env var names in the settings `script_env_allowlist` (intersected with
  the node's `env`), plus `HOME` (always). With an empty allowlist there is **no
  `PATH`**, so external binaries (`python3`, `mkdir`, `cat`, …) are not found. A
  self-contained script command must set its own PATH, e.g.
  `export PATH=/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin` as its first line.
- Set `cwd` to a directory that **already exists**. The executor `chdir`s before
  running, so pointing `cwd` at a directory the command itself is supposed to
  create fails before the command runs. Use a stable dir (e.g. `/tmp`) and have
  the command create its own subdirectories with absolute paths.
- `agent_task` nodes are **not** subject to `scripts_enabled` or the env
  allowlist — they run with the full process environment (so host credentials
  like `gh` auth are available there, not in script nodes).

## Delivery: workflows do not auto-send

A cron *prompt* job auto-delivers the agent's final message to its `--deliver`
target. A *workflow* does not: a node's output is not sent anywhere by itself.
To deliver a message (chat, Telegram, etc.) a node must send it explicitly
(e.g. `hermes send --to <target> --file <path>`). The robust pattern is: a
worker node writes the finished message to a file, and a separate downstream
node sends that file — so a delivery failure does not lose the completed work,
and the send can be retried or routed to a failure path independently.

## Cron-triggering a workflow

Run a workflow on a schedule the plugin-native way, not via a parallel
prompt-cron:

- Declare `trigger: {type: cron, schedule: "<5-field cron, UTC>"}` in the spec,
  or register the trigger through the plugin. Either compiles into a native
  **no-agent** cron job named `workflow:<id>` whose shim execs
  `hermes-workflows run <id> [--origin <deliver>]`.
- The shim lives under `HERMES_HOME/scripts/`; the cron job carries the schedule
  and an optional delivery target threaded onto the run as its origin.
- Do not keep a separate prompt-based cron doing the same job — one trigger per
  workflow.

## Release scopes, resume, and template export

- For release workflows that drive existing board cards, use `adopt: true` with
  `stack: true` and the shared release branch. Do not ask per-card workers to
  bump versions or edit CHANGELOG; one final docs-version node owns that for the
  full locked scope.
- Resume a failed workflow with `resume <run_id>` when exactly one node failed,
  `resume <run_id> --node <id>` when choosing a failed node explicitly, or
  `resume <run_id> --all` to restart a failed or cancelled run from scratch.
  Resume reads the current live spec but refuses structural graph drift.
- Use `export --as-template <id>` when sharing a workflow outside this Hermes
  installation. It writes a placeholder `.template.yaml` plus a `.template.md`
  adaptation guide; v1 does not import templates or rewrite prompt bodies.

## Validate before relying on a spec

`validate <spec>` and `compile-preview <spec>` (the core CLI) are the same checks
the editor runs on save. Aim for zero errors and zero warnings before shipping a
spec. A `cycle` warning is benign when the loop is intentional (e.g. a fix→review
loop or a self-polling wait) — confirm the loop terminates.

## Node types

- `agent_task` — an LLM step (a `prompt`, plus `profile`, `model`, `timeout_seconds`).
- `script` — a deterministic shell step (see the `script` node section above).
- `human_review` — a gate that pauses for an operator decision. It declares
  `options` (e.g. `approved` / `rejected` / `needs_changes`); branch out of it
  with `condition: {type: review_status, equals: <option>}`. `review_status`
  branches may be partial — an unhandled option intentionally stops the run.
- `condition` — a routing-only node that resolves instantly.
- `wait` — a worker-free external wait; it parks and is polled by the tick each
  cycle until its predicate settles.
- `finish` — a terminal node carrying `outcome: success|failure`. Give a
  workflow one success finish and one failure finish, and route to them.

## Operator input (manual runs)

- A run started with a free-form operator instruction (`run <id> --input "..."`)
  has that text **layered above every `agent_task` node's prompt** as a
  highest-priority block ("OPERATOR INSTRUCTION - HIGHEST PRIORITY ... --- node
  instructions ---"). It is not a `{{placeholder}}`; it is prepended
  automatically, overrides conflicting node instructions, and otherwise binds as
  an extra constraint. To make a workflow controllable this way, write node
  prompts that anticipate such an instruction and map it onto concrete behaviour
  (e.g. honor a "top N" / time-window scope by adjusting flags and how much of
  the work-list to process). The cron/no-input path runs the full default scope.
- Heavy or specialized reasoning can be delegated from an orchestrating
  `agent_task` to a separate CLI (e.g. `HOME=/root claude -p --model <m> "$(cat
  prompt.md)"`): the node assembles a context-rich prompt, shells out, captures
  output (`tee`), retries once on failure, and parses the result. This keeps the
  mechanical loop on a cheap model while a stronger model does the judgment.

## Passing data between nodes

- `input_mapping` threads an upstream node's output into a prompt placeholder:
  set `input_mapping: {var: "{{nodes.<id>.output}}"}` and reference `{{var}}` in
  the prompt. A human-review gate's free-text note is readable as
  `{{nodes.<gate-id>.review_note}}`. These placeholders are substituted at
  schedule time; single braces (e.g. a JSON literal `{"k": 1}`) are left alone.

## Driving existing board cards (adopt)

- An `agent_task` with `adopt: true` and `task_ref` does not run a fresh prompt —
  it drives EXISTING Kanban cards through the board as the node's work. A failed
  adopt **hard-stops** the run (`abort_run`) and does not fall through to
  downstream nodes; explicit success/failure edges enforce this. This is the one
  case where project scope and per-node cards are the point, not overhead.

## Run lifecycle and operations

- Drive runs with the entrypoint: `run <id>` (start + first advance),
  `status <run-id>`, `cancel <run-id>`, `advance-all`, `advance-run <run-id>`
  (scoped to one run).
- A multi-node run advances **event-driven**: when a worker completes or blocks a
  workflow card it fires the native Kanban lifecycle hooks
  (`kanban_task_completed` / `kanban_task_blocked`, Hermes #50349), which the
  plugin observes to spawn a detached `advance-run <run-id>` in seconds, scoped
  to the owning run. A per-run debounce
  (`plugins.workflows.event_debounce_seconds`, default 2s) coalesces a burst of
  parallel-node completions into one spawn.
- Behind the event path, a singleton **tick** cron (`advance-all`) remains as the
  coarse safety-net + `wait`-node poll, created while runs are active and torn
  down when none remain. Its cadence is the configurable
  `plugins.workflows.tick_schedule` setting (default `every 2m`). A `wait` node
  or a missed event still settles on the next tick, so a run never stalls after
  its first step.
- **Single-flight:** at most one active run per workflow. A stuck or orphaned
  active run blocks new runs (an "active run exists" refusal) — cancel it before
  re-running.
- Where the underlying work is idempotent, a crashed or cancelled run is safe to
  re-run from scratch.
- Note (direct executor): a `global`/direct `agent_task` runs in a detached
  process that outlives whichever short-lived process performed the advance (a
  bare CLI `run` or a CLI tick) and writes its own settled completion, so the
  node no longer hangs `started`-but-unsettled when that process exits.
  Unattended (cron) global execution is supported without a long-lived driver.

## Enforced execution settings

- `execution.default_mode`: `durable` (one node per tick) vs `direct` / `auto`
  (drains inline-eligible steps synchronously within one advance).
- `execution.scripts_enabled` and `execution.script_env_allowlist`: see the
  `script` node section.
- These are enforced (security-relevant) settings. Each `run` / `advance` happens
  in a fresh process that reads them live, so a settings change takes effect on
  the next run without restarting the gateway.

## Memory provider

- `defaults.memory.provider` is `auto | open_second_brain | none`. Use `none` for
  a workflow that must not write to the agent memory / vault layer; `auto` lets
  the host decide. `fail_open: true` keeps a run going when the memory backend is
  unavailable rather than failing the node.
